months and nine days old. This is equivalent to 22
hours and 36 minutes. His G.M.T. of birth is 6-2 a.m. which is
5 hours 58 minutes away from the nearest noon. Subtracting
it from 22.36 we get 16.38. This would give 4-38 a.m. on
23.1.1920. The planetary positions at 4-38 a.m. on 23-1-1920
measure to first January, 1921. Add the required number of
days; and you get the positions at 4-38 a.m. on 25-2-1920 to
stand for 1-1-1953.
These two methods are attempts to bypass a given prob
lem. We take to some farfetched methods to explain away

114 Rectification of Birth Time
some difficulty. But if they are seen to account for a good
many facts, we can try to accept them.
In this connection mention must be made of the attempt of
the Kabalists. They erect a map for the seventh hour after
birth only to judge whether the child will survive. This, of
course, cannot solve our problem.
The Tajaka method of solar revolution does work
very well; and some method for rectifying the given time of
